---
title: "Chapter 1: Introduction"
---

::: {.archive-notice}
**Source:** Pages 17--22 of *MintonThesis.pdf* (September 2009). Text extracted from PDF; figures extracted directly as images.
:::

At its simplest, this thesis is a work of social policy analysis, focussed on understanding the context, effectiveness, and evidence relating to a programme of incapacity benefits (now Employment and Support Allowance[^1]) reform known as 'Pathways to Work'. These tasks are most explicitly the focus of the last four chapters (chapters nine through twelve inclusive). The thesis, however, is about more than this.

[^1]: As of 18 March 2009

Within this thesis, I will explore political decision-making, welfare reform, economic theory (both as imagined and as applied), the function and emergence of the nation state, the concept of employment and unemployment, the ontology of social organisations, habits and heuristics, social security, the use of statistical metrics as means for automating bureaucratic practice, the nature and means of exacting political power, the relationship between employment and health, long-term working-age sickness, notions of fairness and justice, and durable social inequality.

I will do this by first summarising a range of theories about biological, social, and societal phenomena that I consider useful for understanding these issues, as they manifest and implicate themselves at various scales of observation. Then, I will marshal evidence of social and political processes, as they occur at various scales of observation. Chapters 2--6 comprise the 'theory' section, and chapters 7--11 comprise the 'empirical' section. This relationship is shown graphically in @fig-thesis-structure.

![Relationship between thesis sections and chapters](images/fig-001.png){#fig-thesis-structure}

## 1.1 Theory Section

People are products of chemical and biological processes, amongst other things, and as such all of the historical and sociological forces that converge to shape the culture within which an individual is embedded are experienced and imbued with meaning through chemical and neurological structures that developed over countless iterations of evolutionary processes. As just intimated, people are also products of history, culture, and society, and so to attempt to understand (for example) the 'fitness' of a human individual, without reference to the range of culturally constructed 'adaptations' — tools, ideas, and systems of social interdependence — available to that individual is liable to be a foolish endeavour.

In the case of social phenomena, the province of the social sciences, 'big things' cannot be properly understood without an understanding of 'small things', and 'small things' without an understanding of 'big things'. Unfortunately, with the increasing specialisation and professionalization of academic disciplines, these complementary understandings — the 'big thing' scientists' understanding of 'small things', and the 'small thing' scientists' understanding of 'big things' — may be under-developed and insufficient. The more one specialises in one area, the more one has to rely on vague assumptions, intuitions, and common-sense beliefs in other areas. Unlike the specialist topic that one focuses on, where any statements one makes are subject to rigorous appraisal and evaluation by fellow specialists, the general background assumptions that surround the specialism are subject to scant attention, and may be no more developed or accurate than those held by members of society at large.

Within each of the chapters in the theory section, I present what Charles Tilly might have called a 'superior story',[^2] an attempt to synthesise and articulate the results of a broad range of technical accounts into causal narratives accessible to non-specialists. Within these chapters, I present simplified accounts of simplified accounts of findings, theories, evidence, research and ideas emanating from a very broad range of academic disciplines.

[^2]: Tilly, C. (2006). *Why? What happens when people give reasons ... and why.* Oxford, Princeton University Press.

Chapters two to six present, respectively, the following:

> **Chapter Two:** An account of ideas presented within neuroscientist Antonio Damasio's book *Descartes' Error*,[^3] of people-as-organisms; and a more general discussion of the ontology of organisms. (Time scale: microseconds to minutes. Spatial scale: micrometres to centimetres.)

[^3]: Damasio, A. R. (1995). *Descartes' error: emotion, reason and the human brain.* New York, Avon Books.

> **Chapter Three:** An account of ideas developed within a single chapter of biogeographer and physiologist Jared Diamond's book *Guns, Germs and Steel*, speculating on the reasons why and how overtly political structures (such as governments) came into existence, and came to become predominant as societal structures. (Time scale: centuries to millennia. Spatial scale: kilometres to thousands of kilometres.)

> **Chapter Four:** An account of Stanley Milgram's *Obedience to Authority* research (described within his book of the same name), with a view to illustrating how patterns of microbehaviour observed when dyads and triads of people interact can lead to the emergence of the kinds of power structures detailed within chapters three and five. (Time scale: Seconds to hours. Spatial scale: metres to tens of metres.)

> **Chapter Five:** An account of Max Weber's theories of bureaucracy and bureaucratization, with a view to highlighting both the historical specificity, and the pervasive ubiquity of standard ('mundane') bureaucratic practices, and to illustrate how political decision-making becomes translated into large-scale political action. (Time scale: days to decades. Spatial scale: metres to hundreds of kilometres.)

> **Chapter Six:** An account of anthropologist Alan Page Fiske's Relational Models Theory (RMT), highlighting the strong similarities between RMT, and economic historian Karl Polanyi's political theory known as 'substantivism'. (Time scale: minutes to days. Spatial scale: metres to kilometres.)

@fig-theory-chapters broadly indicates how I believe these theories relate to each other.

![Relationship between theory chapters](images/fig-002.png){#fig-theory-chapters}

## 1.2 Evidence Section

Understanding 'Pathways to Work', a programme of reforms to incapacity benefits, was and is at the core of this thesis. It is the initial object of enquiry around which the rest of the thesis accumulated and developed. As I have suggested, I think that properly understanding Pathways also involves exploring and understanding a range of other topics and phenomena of which Pathways is just one aspect. The immediate topic, Pathways to Work, involves understanding processes, such as political decision-making and the ways in which pilot programmes were commissioned and evaluated, that occurred in very recent history, within the last five years, and that extend into the near future. Understanding why the programme reached the stage of being piloted and evaluated, however, involves understanding changes in the labour market, and large scale structural adjustments in the British economy that occurred over the last thirty years. And understanding why these 'adjustments' matter means learning more about the history and application of the concept of 'the labour market', and so learning more about developments that have occurred over the last century.

Evidence relating to the 'five year' topic is presented within chapters nine, ten, and eleven. Evidence relating to the 'thirty year' topic is presented within chapter eight. And evidence relating to the 'hundred year' topic is presented within chapter seven. Graphically, this relationship is illustrated in @fig-evidence-chapters.

![Relationship between evidence chapters](images/fig-003.png){#fig-evidence-chapters}

## 1.3 Summary and Conclusion

Within the evidence section, I rely heavily (though not exhaustively) on statistics and methods of statistical inference. If forced to attempt to summarise the thesis in a single sentence, however, I might describe it as "a story about how some statistics take on a life of their own." Though fairly technical in places, the extent to which statistical inference is formalised is far less than would be normal within a number of social science disciplines. (Economics, for instance.)

If it were formalised to the extent usually expected of academic research pursued within many of the social science disciplines that emphasise the subject over the predicate ('social sciences'), the 'five year' topic of the last two or three chapters could very conceivably have been expanded into a thesis in its own right. I hope that, over the course of reading this thesis, the reasons why breadth of investigation has occasionally been pursued at the expense of depth are understood, and that the comparative merits of this broad 'multiple-perspective' approach become evident.
